correct_answer: The impact of the medication on bone density may not correlate with the risk for fracture
reasoning: I don't agree that "A" is the correct answer. Women start loosing bone after 30...that's a fact. A decreased bone density on a DEXA scan correlates with an increased risk for fracture. An Unchanged bone density score over a 5 year period correlates shows that the patient has maintained the same amount of bone density over time instead of losing it which is a natural occurence as we age.  Being that the participants did not lose any bone over a 5 year period, the risk of fracture fot he participant is the same on day one of the study as it is at the conclusion of the study 5 years later after taking the osteoperosis medication. What is not addressed in this scenario is the diet and exercise program for each participant in the study. A calcium rich diet, normal vitamin D levels and low impact bone building exercise such as walking, weight lifting and aerobic exercises play a big role in the management of osteoperosis.  Without knowing each participants lifestyle, the Primary Investigator of the study should not definativly conclude that the new medication is the reason for stable bone density and decreases the risk for fracture in women aged 40 to 60 years. The study subjects diet and lifestyle can be a confounding variable
